coredata.dc:title: Design of solid-state sodium-ion batteries with high mass-loading cathode by porous-dense bilayer electrolyte
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Chinese Ceramic Society. Production and hosting by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Chinese Ceramic Society. Production and hosting by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Lai, Hongjian
Li, Yanpei
Wang, Jingyi
Li, WenWen
Wu, Xiangwei
Wen, Zhaoyin
coredata.dc:description: Solid-state sodium-ion batteries with sodium metal anodes possess high safety and reliability, which are considered as a promising candidate for the next generation of energy storage technology. However, poor electronic and ionic conductivities at the interface between electrodes and solid-state electrolytes restrict its practical application. Herein, we demonstrate a β″-Al2O3 electrolyte with a vertically porous-dense bilayer structure to solve this problem. The carbon-coated vertically porous layer serves as a high mass-loading host for Na3V2(PO4)3 cathode and provides fast electronically and ionically conductive pathways. In addition, the dense layer is produced to prevent sodium dendrite growth and improve mechanical strength of β″-Al2O3 electrolyte. Experimental results show that the cathode loading in vertically porous layer can reach to 8 mg cm−2, and the porous-dense bilayer β″-Al2O3 electrolyte-based battery exhibits a reversible specific capacity of 87 mAh g−1 and a capacity retention of 95.5% over 100 cycles at a current density of 0.1 C, which is superior to that of the traditional dense β″-Al2O3 electrolyte-based battery. This work based on electrolyte structure design represents an efficient strategy for the development of solid-state sodium-ion batteries with high mass-loading cathode.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Solid-state sodium-ion batteries
Electrolyte structure design
Porous-dense bilayer β″-Al2O3 electrolyte
High mass-loading cathode

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of β″-Al2O3 electrolyte with porous-dense bilayer structure

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the bilayer β″-Al2O3 electrolyte structure with high mass-loading NVP cathode.

(a) Schematic of the bilayer β″-Al2O3 electrolyte. (b) The vertically porous β″-Al2O3 layer was prepared by the ice-templet method (c) The dense β″-Al2O3 layer was obtained by dipping (d) Cross-sectional morphologies of the bilayer β″-Al2O3 electrolyte with a total thickness of 1050 μm consist of vertically porous layer of 960 μm and dense layer of 90 μm (e) Magnification of the cross-section (f) Cross-sectional and surface morphologies of the vertically porous β″-Al2O3 layer (g) the breaking force of the vertically porous layer of 960 μm compared to the 960 μm vertically porous and 90 μm dense bilayer. (h) XRD of the vertically porous layer and dense layer. (i) Arrhenius plots of the dense β″-Al2O3 pellet.

Characterization of the bilayer β″-Al2O3 electrolyte with NVP cathode. (a) photograph and (b) SEM and (c) EDS of the carbon coated vertically porous layer. (d) XRD and (e) SEM and (f) TEM of the NVP synthesized by a sol-gel method. (g), (h) SEM and (i) DES of cross-sectional morphologies of the vertically porous β″-Al2O3 layer with NVP cathode.

Electrochemical characterization of the bilayer β″-Al2O3 electrolyte. (a) Voltage profiles and (b) Cycle performance of the bilayer β″-Al2O3 electrolyte and NVP loading of 8 mg cm−2. (c) Voltage profile and (d) EIS compares the bilayer β″-Al2O3 electrolyte and the dense β″-Al2O3 electrolyte with different NVP loadings. (e) Cycle performance of the dense β″-Al2O3 electrolyte and NVP loading of 6.5 mg cm−2.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: Peer review under responsibility of The Chinese Ceramic Society.

head.ce:title: Design of solid-state sodium-ion batteries with high mass-loading cathode by porous-dense bilayer electrolyte

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory of Materials for Energy Conversion

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Solid-state sodium-ion batteries with sodium metal anodes possess high safety and reliability, which are considered as a promising candidate for the next generation of energy storage technology. However, poor electronic and ionic conductivities at the interface between electrodes and solid-state electrolytes restrict its practical application. Herein, we demonstrate a β″-Al2O3 electrolyte with a vertically porous-dense bilayer structure to solve this problem. The carbon-coated vertically porous layer serves as a high mass-loading host for Na3V2(PO4)3 cathode and provides fast electronically and ionically conductive pathways. In addition, the dense layer is produced to prevent sodium dendrite growth and improve mechanical strength of β″-Al2O3 electrolyte. Experimental results show that the cathode loading in vertically porous layer can reach to 8 mg cm−2, and the porous-dense bilayer β″-Al2O3 electrolyte-based battery exhibits a reversible specific capacity of 87 mAh g−1 and a capacity retention of 95.5% over 100 cycles at a current density of 0.1 C, which is superior to that of the traditional dense β″-Al2O3 electrolyte-based battery. This work based on electrolyte structure design represents an efficient strategy for the development of solid-state sodium-ion batteries with high mass-loading cathode.

ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical secondary batteries have been considered as one of the most effective energy storage technologies in recent years [1,2]. Among them, lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have attracted great attentions because of its high power density, high energy density and long cycle life, which have been successfully applied in portable electronic devices and electric vehicles [3,4]. However, the limited abundance and uneven distribution of lithium resources hinder the large-scale applications of LIBs. Hence, it is imperative to develop alternative secondary batteries replacements of LIBs in the future [5]. Sodium-ion batteries (SIBs) are considered to be one of the most promising candidates due to abundant sodium resources, low cost and similar performance as LIBs [6,7]. Nevertheless, traditional SIBs using flammable organic liquid electrolytes are still suffering from safety challenges. In this case, solid electrolytes are proposed to replace liquid electrolytes to improve safety and reliability of the batteries [8–10]. Sodium solid-state electrolytes can be classified into ceramic electrolytes [11,12] and polymer electrolytes [13]. Among them, layer structured β″-Al2O3 ceramic is considered as one of the most potential electrolytes for practical applications due to its high ionic conductivity, high density and good stability against sodium [14,15]. The most successful demonstrations are sodium-sulfur [16,17] and sodium-nickel chloride batteries which use β″-Al2O3 as the electrolyte [18,19].

Like the solid-state LIBs, the solid-state SIBs also suffer poor interfacial contact between solid electrolytes and electrodes. Besides, the mass-loading of cathode for the traditional dense β″-Al2O3 electrolyte-based battery is limited due to poor electronically and ionically conductive pathways [20]. In response to these problems, many studies have been devoted [21]. Recently, Liu et al. [22] mixed Na0.66Ni0.33Mn0.67O2 particles with N-Alkyl-N-methylpyrrolidinium-bis(fluorosulfonyl)imide ionic liquid to form a toothpaste-like cathode which could easily adhere to β″-Al2O3 electrolyte for improving ionic conductivity, which delivered a specific capacity of 80 mAh g −1 in voltage range of 2.5–3.8 V at 0.1 C. Zhao et al. [23] assembled solid-state battery consisting of a ceramic β″-Al2O3 as the electrolyte with a NaTi2(PO4)3 gel composite cathode layer for improving the interfacial contact, which exhibited an extremely stable voltage plateau of approximately 2.1 V. Although much progress has been realized, poor electronic and ionic conductivities at the interface still exist and need to be solved urgently.

In this study, a novel vertically orientation structure is proposed to facilitate electron and ion diffusion kinetics. The distinct merits of orientation structure are large porosity, the low diffusion tortuosity coefficient(τ) and high dynamic effective diffusion coefficient (D eff). Therefore, we propose a new β″-Al2O3 electrolyte with vertically porous-dense bilayer structure. The carbon-coated vertically porous β″-Al2O3 layer was utilized as cathode framework, which can provide fast electronically and ionically conductive pathways for the high mass-loading encapsulated cathode, while the dense layer can prevent sodium dendrite penetration and increase mechanical strength of β″-Al2O3 electrolyte, as schematically illustrated in Fig. 1 . The vertically porous β″-Al2O3 layer was prepared by a facile ice-templet method [24], and the dense β″-Al2O3 layer was obtained by slurry impregnation method. Glucose was infiltrated into the vertically porous layer, which was carbonized subsequently to form an electron conduction pathway. Na3V2(PO4)3(NVP) [25,26] was selected as the cathode material and added to the vertically porous layer. The experimental results show that the vertically porous layer have a high porosity (46%), and the mass-loading of NVP cathode can reach as high as 8 mg cm−2. The solid-state SIB exhibits excellent electrochemical performance with a high specific capacity of 87 mAh g−1 and a capacity retention of 95.5% over 100 cycles at 0.1 C. The results suggest that solid-state electrolytes with porous-dense bilayer structure are promising for fabricating solid-state SIBs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of β″-Al2O3 electrolyte with porous-dense bilayer structure
ce:section.ce:para: The β″-Al2O3 powders were prepared by a solid-state reaction method and dense β″-Al2O3 pellets were prepared by cold isostatic pressing according to our reported technique [27]. The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image of dense β″-Al2O3 pellet is shown in Fig. S1. The vertically porous β″-Al2O3 layer was prepared by a facile ice-templet method. Firstly, the β″-Al2O3 powders, tert-butyl alcohol and polyethylene glycol were mixed to form the slurry with relatively low viscosity. Secondly, a sponge template with a diameter of 18 mm was used to absorb the above slurry with low viscosity, and it was frozen on a semiconductor refrigeration plate at −50 °C for 1 h. Then, the ice was evaporated in a freeze dryer. Lastly, the obtained sample was calcined in a furnace at 1200 °C for two hours to form the backbone with porous structure. The β″-Al2O3 powders with triethanolamine as a dispersant were dissolved into a mixed solution of alcohol and butanone. After ball milling for 4 h, binder (polyvinyl butyral) and plasticizer (polyethylene glycol) were added into above solution with further milling to obtain the paste-like slurry with relatively high viscosity, which was used for the preparation of dense β″-Al2O3 layer. One side of the porous backbone was impregnated with the above slurry repeatedly. After evaporating the solvent, the obtained sample was co-sintered at 1550 °C for 10 min to form the β″-Al2O3 electrolyte with porous-dense bilayer structure.

Na3V2(PO4)3(NVP) was synthesized by a sol-gel method as reported previously [28]. Firstly, V2O5 and H2C2O4 were dissolved into distilled water and stirred at 80 °C for 30 min. Then, NaH2PO4 and C6H8O7 were added to the solution with violent stirring until a clear sol form, which was dried at 150 °C to obtain the gel. Lastly, the gel was heated at 800 °C in argon atmosphere for 8 h to obtain NVP.

The saturated glucose solution was infiltrated into the vertically porous layer and was sintered in 800 °C for 8 h to form carbon coated vertically porous structure. The as-synthesized NVP was dissolved in N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) and ball-milled for 6 h to obtain the slurry of cathode. The slurry was poured into the vertically porous layer and dried in a vacuum oven to evaporate NMP solvent, which was repeated several times until the expected mass-loading was reached in the vertically porous layer [29]. Then a small amount of liquid electrolyte was added to wet the interface [30]. Finally, a sodium metal foil was attached to the anodic side of dense layer and the porous layer was covered by an aluminum foil. For comparison, a slurry of NVP which consisted of active material (80 wt%), super P (10 wt%) and polytetrafluoroethylene (10 wt%) was completely dispersed in NMP. The above slurry was evaporated and then pressed onto a steel mesh to obtain the cathode. The as-prepared cathode was dried at 80 °C for 12 h in a vacuum oven. Sodium metal foil and dense β″-Al2O3 pellet were used as anode and electrolyte, respectively. And a small amount of liquid electrolyte was used to wet the interface. All the operations of assembling the battery were performed in the glove box with argon.

X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) using a Rigaku Ultima IV with Cu-Ka radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å) was used to analyze the phase of samples. Scanning electron microscope (SEM, S-3400 N) and transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100F) were conducted to observe the morphologies of samples and energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) was applied to investigate the elemental distributions of the samples. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed in a PerkinElmer-DSC 8000 with a heating rate of 5 °C min−1 from 50 to 700 °C under O2 atmosphere. Porosity was measured by Archimedes buoyancy method. In accordance with ASTM international testing standards, the breaking force of porous-dense bilayer β″-Al2O3 electrolytes were measured by three-point bending test with a mechanical strength tester (Instron 3366).

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurement was carried out using an electrochemical workstation (Autolab PGSTAT302) with an amplitude of 10 mV from 0.1 Hz to 1 MHz. The charge and discharge tests were measured with a Land battery tester (CT2001A) in the potential range of 2.8–4.0 V.

The ionic conductivity (σ) of the dense β″-Al2O3 electrolytes were obtained by the equation in various temperature:
σ =
L
R S
L and S represent the thickness and the area of the electrolytes respectively and were measured by vernier caliper. R represents the impedance of the electrolyte.

The schematic of a novel β″-Al2O3 electrolyte with porous-dense bilayer structure is illustrated in Fig. 2 a. The vertically porous layer was successfully synthesized by a facile ice-templet method as shown in Fig. 2b. To achieve a temperature gradient, the bottom of template was placed in a cold source (−50 °C) and the top was exposed at ambient atmosphere (25 °C). Vertical pores were formed by ice directional growth under the great thermal gradient. Then, β″-Al2O3 pellet with vertically porous structure was formed by freeze-drying and solid-sintering. Then the one side of porous layer was impregnated with the β″-Al2O3 slurry with higher viscosity, as schemed in Fig. 2c β″-Al2O3 electrolyte with vertically porous-dense bilayer structure was finally prepared by co-sintering of the as-obtained precursor. More details of the preparation process are described in the experimental section.

Fig. 2d presents the SEM image of the cross-section of the bilayer β″-Al2O3 electrolyte. As seen, the total thickness is about 1050 μm, including the vertically porous layer of 960 μm and dense layer of 90 μm. It was found in Fig. 2e that the vertically porous layer and dense layer are closely connected. Fig. 2f shows the SEM image of the vertically porous layer. The diameter of the pore is in the range of 10–20 μm. The porous layer has a porosity as high as 46% measured by Archimedes method (Fig. S2), which is beneficial to accommodate high mass-loading cathode. The crystal grains of ceramic framework are tightly combined so that it can provide continuous ionically conductive pathways, in favor of improving the capacity performance of the cathode. Compared with the traditional dense β″-Al2O3 electrolyte,the vertically porous β″-Al2O3 electrolyte has large specific surface area to improve the effective availability of cathode, and it is also lighter when the volume is the same (Fig. S3). The dense layer without cracks (Fig. S4) can effectively prevent sodium dendrite growth. Additionally, it can also increase the mechanical strength of the β″-Al2O3 electrolyte. Fig. 2g shows the breaking force of the porous layer and bilayer composite electrolyte respectively. The breaking force of the vertically porous layer of 960 μm was only 4 N. In contrast, the breaking force of the bilayer of 960 μm vertically porous and 90 μm dense bilayer can reach 15 N. Obviously, the dense layer of 90 μm can dramatically enhance mechanical property of β″-Al2O3 electrolyte. The X-ray diffraction was used to analyze the phases of the porous layer and dense layer in Fig. 2h. The result shows that both are adapt well to pure β″ -Al2O3 phase and no impurity phase is introduced during freeze-drying process. In addition, the β″ -Al2O3 of vertically porous structure has good crystallinity. Fig. 2i shows the Arrhenius plots of the dense β″-Al2O3 pellets under the same sintering conditions. It can be calculated that the ionic conductivity of β″-Al2O3 electrolyte can reach 3.2 × 10−3 S cm−1 at 60 °C (Fig. S5).

Saturated glucose solution was infiltrated into the vertically porous and carbonized to form continuous carbon networks, which can enhance the electronic conductivity at the interface between electrode and electrolyte. Fig. 3 a shows the optical photograph of the carbon-coated vertically porous layer. The SEM and EDS images of the porous layer before and after carbon coating are given in Fig. S6 and Fig. 3b, c, indicating that the carbon covers the pore walls uniformly. NVP cathode was synthesized by a sol-gel method. Fig. 3d shows the X-ray diffraction spectrum of NVP, which can be indexed to a well-crystalline structure (PDF #33-1296). Fig. 3e presents the SEM image of NVP sample. It can be observed that the particle size is about 200 nm on average. And the TEM image shows the uniform amorphous carbon coating on the surface of NVP particles in Fig. 3f. In addition, the amount of carbon is estimated to be about 17 wt% and displayed in the TGA curve (Fig. S7). In order to realize the high mass-loading cathode, NVP was infiltrated into the vertically porous layer and repeatedly until mass-loading of 8 mg cm−2 was reached. The cross-section of the cathode and elemental mapping are shown in Fig. 3g, h and i. It can be observed that NVP is successfully distributed in the pores of the vertically porous layer. And the volume expansion of NVP can be alleviated by the porous framework during charge and discharge process. Moreover, a small amount of liquid electrolyte is added to improve contact between the vertically porous layer and NVP cathode for reducing the interfacial impedance.

The solid-state SIBs with a high mass-loading NVP of 8 mg cm−2 were prepared. All electrochemical tests of batteries were carried out at 60 °C. The discharge and charge voltage profiles of the full cells were performed at a current rate of 0.1 C in the voltage range of 2.8–4.0 V. As seen in Fig. 4 a, the battery delivers a discharge specific capacity of around 87 mAh g−1. The flat and long voltage plateaus of 3.4 V indicate the uncompromised polarization between the discharge and charge curves, which is attributed to the vertically porous layer with large specific surface area to increase the number of cathode reactive sites. Herein, the as-prepared battery exhibits a low voltage polarization and a high specific capacity. Moreover, the shapes of the voltage curves remain almost constant and no significant voltage drop is observed during repeated charge and discharge process of 100 cycles, which indicates that the battery designed by porous-dense bilayer electrolyte structure possesses excellent electrochemical stability. And the volume expansion of NVP can be alleviated by the vertically porous framework during charge and discharge process.

Fig. 4b shows cycling performance of the battery. After 100 cycles, the discharge capacity remains 83.1 mAh g−1 with a retention of 95.5%, which demonstrates the satisfactory cycling stability of the battery. For comparison, we also prepared traditional battery with dense β″-Al2O3 pellet as the electrolyte. Fig. 4c compares the voltage profiles during charge-discharge process of the batteries with different electrolytes and mass-loadings. The battery with bilayer β″-Al2O3 as the electrolyte and NVP loading of 8 mg cm−2 can deliver higher area capacity of 0.70 mAh cm−2, whereas the battery with dense β″-Al2O3 pellet and NVP loading of 0.8 (6.5) mg cm−2 can only deliver an area capacity of 0.05 (0.43) mAh cm−2 at 0.1 C. The former also shows superior performance of low interfacial impedance. As shown in Fig. 4d, the impedance of the battery with bilayer β″-Al2O3 is about 115 Ω, 1/20 of that of the battery with dense β″-Al2O3 pellet. The low impedance is due to good contact between the cathode and bilayer β″-Al2O3 electrolyte, and the construction of three dimensional electronic and ionic conductive networks in the porous layer. In addition, for the battery with dense β″-Al2O3 pellet as the electrolyte, the capacity retention of NVP loading with 0.8 mg cm−2 is only 75% after 50 cycles (Fig. S8). And when NVP loading reaches 6.5 mg cm−2, the capacity undergoes a rapid capacity fading after 10 cycles and the battery fails after 35 cycles as shown in Fig. 4e. By comparison, the battery with bilayer β″-Al2O3 as the electrolyte shows much better electrochemical performance, which is attributed that the bilayer β″-Al2O3 can provide highly continuous and interconnected transport channels to improve electronic and ionic conductivities and increase connection between electrode and solid electrolyte.

In this work, we successfully prepared β″-Al2O3 electrolyte with porous-dense bilayer structure. This work demonstrates that bilayer structure can possess higher cathode mass-loading and provide fast electronically and ionically conductive pathways. Results show that the battery designed by the porous-dense composite electrolyte alleviates the interfacial impedance and delivers enhanced electrochemical performance compared with that of the battery with dense β″-Al2O3 pellet. Our methodology may provide a promising construction strategy for solid-state batteries with high energy density.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) project No. 51672300, opening project of CAS Key Laboratory of Materials for Energy Conversion.

Hu, Three-dimensional bilayer garnet solid electrolyte based high energy density lithium metal-sulfur batteries, Energy & Environmental Science 10(7) (2017) 1568-1575. https://doi.org/10.1039/c7ee01004d.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Hongjian Lai:is currently a Ph.D. student in Dr. Zhaoyin Wen’s group at the Shanghai Institute of Ceramics, Chinese Academy of Sciences (SICCAS). He received B.S. from Liaoning University in 2018. His research mainly focuses on solid-state sodium-ion batteries. Email addresses: laihongjian@student.sic.ac.cn

Zhaoyin Wen:obtained his B.S. at the Nanjing University of Chemical Technology, China in 1984 and Ph.D. from the Shanghai Institute of Ceramics, Chinese Academy of Sciences in 1998. He worked on solid electrolytes for lithium-ion batteries at Mie University, Japan from 1999 to 2001 as a visiting professor. Now his research interests focused on secondary batteries, including lithium-sulfur, lithium-air, sodium-sulfur, and sodium chloride batteries. He has published more than 270 scientific papers which received more than 5000 citations from other researches. Email addresses: zywen@mail.sic.ac.cn

Xiangwei Wu:received his Ph.D. from Shanghai Institute of Ceramics, Chinese Academy of Sciences (SICCAS) in 2009. He is now a researcher in SICCAS. His current research focuses on sodium-based solid batteries. Email addresses: xwwu@mail.sic.ac.cn
